message:
fix(ffs-btree): reject zero-length B-tree extent inserts (bd-my4rx)

The fresh-eyes ffs-btree audit found that the public insert() path
accepts a new extent with actual_len(raw_len) == 0, writes it into
the tree, and leaves the corruption to be reported by later
traversal/search calls — i.e., the API itself produced the
corruption it later flags. Both the written and unwritten encodings
share this gap because actual_len() peels off the unwritten flag
before the length test.

This commit threads a single validate_insert_extent() check into
insert() immediately after the header parse/validate but before any
mutation: zero-length extents now bounce out with
FfsError::InvalidGeometry naming the rejected logical block. The
helper is a private fn at the bottom of the lengths section, next
to actual_len() and the encode helpers, so it stays close to the
length-encoding logic it guards.

Regression test
insert_rejects_zero_length_extent_without_mutating_root pins three
invariants:

  * the call returns Err(FfsError::InvalidGeometry(_));
  * the formatted message identifies "zero-length extent" and the
    offending logical block (7 in the test);
  * the root buffer is byte-identical to its pre-call state, the
    SeqAllocator's next pointer is unchanged, and a follow-up
    search() at the same logical block still reports
    SearchResult::Hole — i.e., the failed insert leaves the tree
    indistinguishable from before.

The unwritten-encoding case is intentionally covered by the same
helper: actual_len() strips the unwritten flag before testing for
zero, so an Ext4Extent with raw_len = EXT_INIT_MAX_LEN | UNWRITTEN
that decodes to length 0 (which only happens if a future encoding
bug ever flips the unwritten bit on a zero-length extent) takes the
same rejection path. No second test is needed because the unwritten
bit lives outside the length test by construction.

bd-my4rx is opened to in_progress in 3210ecf and will close once
the full ffs-harness conformance run lands cleanly under rch.
